name: UploadThing API
what_it_does: UploadThing provides file upload infrastructure with a typed file router defined server-side, enabling clients to upload files directly to cloud storage with access control, type validation, and metadata callbacks without exposing storage credentials to the client.
use_cases: Uploading user-submitted files (images, documents, videos) from a web app to cloud storage without building custom upload endpoints
Enforcing file type and size limits declaratively in a file router before accepting uploads
Triggering server-side callbacks after file upload completes to process or persist metadata in the agent workflow
Generating presigned upload URLs server-side for secure direct-to-storage client uploads
Building file upload flows in Next.js or React apps with minimal infrastructure setup
not_for: Non-web or server-to-server file uploads where the client-side SDK adds no value
Large-scale media processing pipelines requiring transformation, CDN delivery, or DAM features (use Cloudinary instead)
Enterprise file storage requiring detailed access controls, versioning, or compliance workflows
best_when: A Next.js or React application needs to add typed, secure file uploads in minutes without managing S3 buckets, presigned URLs, or file size/type validation infrastructure.
avoid_when: You need image transformations, CDN optimization, or are building outside the JavaScript/TypeScript ecosystem.

auth.notes: Single API key from uploadthing.com dashboard used server-side only. Client-side upload uses short-lived tokens issued by the server-side file router — storage credentials are never exposed to the browser. The file router also supports custom middleware for per-request auth checks (e.g., validate session before allowing upload).

pricing.free_tier_limits: 2 GB storage, 5 GB bandwidth per month on free tier
pricing.paid_tiers: Pro: $10/month for 10 GB storage + 50 GB bandwidth; scales with usage
pricing.notes: Simple storage + bandwidth pricing model. Free tier is sufficient for development and small projects. Paid plans are straightforward compared to credit-based systems like Cloudinary.

performance.rate_limits: Not explicitly documented; no known hard limits for uploads at typical scale

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Errors are returned as structured JSON with a code and message. TypeScript SDK surfaces type errors at compile time which reduces runtime surprises. Server-side router errors are logged but client-facing error messages can be generic.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency key support. Re-submitting the same file creates a duplicate object in storage. Agents should track uploaded file keys to avoid duplicates.
known_agent_gotchas: File router must be deployed server-side — agents cannot use UploadThing in a purely serverless function without a persistent router endpoint
Upload tokens are short-lived and tied to the requesting session — agents cannot pre-generate tokens for later use
No built-in deduplication — uploading the same file twice produces two separate stored objects with different keys
Rate limits are undocumented — high-frequency agent uploads may encounter unspecified throttling
SDK is JavaScript/TypeScript only — non-JS agents must use the raw HTTP API which has less documentation coverage

security_score_components.security_notes: API key is server-side only by design — the SDK architecture enforces that clients never see storage credentials. However, scope granularity is limited (one API key, no per-operation scopes). Open-source codebase allows security review.

score_rationale: UploadThing earns high marks for developer experience and security design — the file router pattern keeps credentials server-side and the TypeScript-first SDK eliminates entire categories of runtime errors. However, it is narrowly optimized for the Next.js/React ecosystem, has no image transformation capabilities, undocumented rate limits, and limited compliance certifications. Best as a drop-in upload layer for JS apps, not a general-purpose media management solution.
